mainmatter.p: The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C is a proposition, which we depict in the language of telescopes below:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function between sets that is both an embedding and a surjection is an equivalence. We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:

By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types:
By the univalence principle for functions, the telescope above contracts to \mathopen {} \left [ \, \right ] \mathclose {}.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } } {D \cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
A functor F \colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f \colon C \cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

ol.li: a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert of objects, written c \in C;
for each x,y \in C a set C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} of arrows, written f \colon x \to y;
for each x \in C, an identity arrow 1_{ x } \colon x \to x;
for each f \colon x \to y and g \colon y \to z, a composite arrow g \circ f \colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
f \circ 1_{ x } = f \quad 1_{ y } \circ f = f \quad h \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathopen {} \left ( h \circ g \right ) \mathclose {} \circ f
